content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 22, 2016

Jonathan W. Smith
Deputy City Attorney
1017 Middlefield Road
Redwood City, CA 94063

Re:     Your Request for Advice
         Our File No. A-16-102

Dear Mr. Smith:

         This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Planning Commissioner Ernie Schmidt regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

         Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTIONS

1. May Commissioner Schmidt participate in upcoming decisions pertaining to the following projects (discussed in detail below): (1) the Habitat for Humanity project; (2) the Anton development; (3) the MidPen development; (4) the Greystar development; and (5) the Acclaim development, despite his employment with Golden Fox Venues ("GFV") which operates entertainment venues in the downtown area?

2. Will the the contract between GFV and the City to conduct large, outdoor events in the City's Courthouse Square create a conflict of interest under any of the decisions set forth above?

CONCLUSIONS

1. The Commissioner may make and participate in decisions related to the following: (1) the Habitat for Humanity project; (2) the Anton development; and (3) the MidPen development. However, based on the scope of the Greystar development and the Acclaim development and potential impacts on his employer, the Commissioner may not participate in these developments.

_________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-102
Page No. 2

2. None of the decisions related to any of the projects in question would appear to affect the
arrangement between the City and GFV or the revenue that GFV receives. Thus, the contractual
arrangement regarding the Courthouse Square would not create a conflict of interest in any of the
decisions described above.

FACTS

You stated that Redwood City is experiencing rapid growth in its downtown area, and
several development projects are expected to come before the Planning Commission in the
foreseeable future. Development in the downtown area is governed by the DTPP. The DTPP
establishes land use, development, and urban design regulations for the 138-acre DTPP area. It sets
Maximum Allowable Development caps ("MAD Caps") of 2,500 net new dwelling units; 100,000
square feet of net new retail development; 500,000 square feet of net new commercial office space;
and 200 net new lodging rooms. For each project, any net new units and/or square footage are
subtracted from the amount available for other projects under the relevant MAD Cap.

As of May 11, 2016, after accounting for all approved projects, the following development
units are left:

• 892 net new dwelling units;

• 99,343 square feet of net new retail development;

• 0 square feet of net new commercial office space; and

• 200 net new lodging rooms remained available under the MAD Caps.

You stated that most of the projects expected to come before the Planning Commission
involve some combination of residential and retail uses. There are enough remaining residential
dwelling units and retail square feet of development under the relevant MAD Caps to allow for each
of the proposed residential and retail projects. Because no commercial office space remains
available under the MAD Cap, no new office space may be developed unless (a) new square
footage becomes available through removal of current office space elsewhere in the DTPP area or
(b) the City Council increases the commercial office space MAD Cap through an amendment to the
DTPP. One expected project includes new office space, and a DTPP amendment will be required
for that project.

Commissioner Schmidt serves as the Director of Marketing and Sales for GFV, a for-profit
company that owns and operates multiple entertainment spaces in downtown Redwood City. The
Commissioner's aggregate income from GFV over the previous 12 months exceeds $500. He does
not have any ownership interest in the business entity or in any GFV real property. GFV owns and
operates the following:

• The largest of these venues is the Fox Theatre, an historic theater that hosts concerts, plays,
galas, and other large events. The Fox Theatre has a capacity of approximately 1,175 guests
and frequently hosts nationally touring artists and ensembles.

File No. A-16-102
Page No. 3

• GFV also operates Club Fox, which is a smaller performance venue at the same location as the Fox Theatre that features concerts, local jam sessions, and other events. Club Fox offers bar and alcohol services to its guests and has a capacity of approximately 240 guests.

The Fox Theatre and Club Fox are located on a single parcel at 2215 Broadway Street in Redwood City (the "Fox Theatre Parcel").

• A third GFV venue, the Fox Forum, offers banquet hall space for events with up to 400 guests. The Fox Forum is located at 2411 Broadway Street.

• GFV also coordinates with the City of Redwood City to create large, outdoor events on Redwood City's historic courthouse square, which is across the street from the GFV venues. GFV receives revenue from these events on Courthouse Square. Redwood City's historic courthouse square is located at 2200 Broadway Street.

On June 10, 2016, you clarified that GFV helps coordinate large events that use both the Fox Theatre Space and the historic courthouse square. For most events, GFV will collect the full amount of rent for both the Fox Theatre and Courthouse square as an administrative convenience. GFV retains the full revenue associated with renting the Theatre (approximately $6000 plus costs) and remits the full amount of rent associated with Courthouse Square (approximately $3,900 plus costs) to the City. In other words, for a typical event, GFV collects the entire rent for both the Theatre and the Square, but it only earns the revenue associated with rental of the Theatre. There is one exception to this general arrangement. Customers may rent a tent from the City for events on Courthouse Square at a rate of $15,960, which GFV collects from the Customer. For such tent rentals, GFV retains a 10% finders fee of $1,596 and sends the remaining $14,364 to the City. Such tent rentals occur approximately twice a year.

The vast majority of sales at the GFV venues in Redwood City come through advance, online ticket sales and reservations rather than from walk-up business. In addition to its Redwood City Venues, GFV operates the Golden State Fox Theatre in Monterey, California.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. (Section 87103.) Section 87103 defines interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. They include the following:

• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

File No. A-16-102
Page No. 4

• Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.

GFV is an interest of the Commissioner as his employer and a source of income. (Section 87103(c) and (d).)

However, a conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. Note that the foreseeability and materiality analysis must be conducted on a decision-by-decision basis, unless the decisions are inextricably interrelated.²

Foreseeability: The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. Regulation 18701 provides: "A financial effect ... is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency." None of the Commissioner's financial interests are explicitly involved in the decisions in question. Thus, pursuant to Regulation 18701(b), if a financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable, however, if the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable.

Materiality: Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the standard for materiality applicable to the effect of a decision on an official's interest in a source of income. Regulation 18702.3(a)(4) provides that if the source of income is a business entity, materiality is instead determined pursuant to the standard for materiality for the effect of a decision on an official's interest in a business entity contained in Regulation 18702.1. Regulation 18702.1 provides (for a business interest not explicitly involved in the decision)³ that the financial effect is material if the source is a business entity and a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the value of a privately-held business entity.

The Residential Projects

1. Habitat for Humanity, Greater San Francisco, has applied for a Planned Community Permit to construct a six-story, 25,000 square foot residential building at 612 Jefferson Avenue. The project would consist of approximately 20 affordable housing units, and Habitat for Humanity will submit a related affordable housing plan for approval to the Planning Commission. The project may not meet the DTPP parking requirements and may require an allowance for such under provision of on-site parking. The project site is 892 feet away from the Fox Theater parcel and 1,312 feet from the Fox Forum.

² Decisions are "inextricably interrelated" when the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision (Regulation 18706(b).) Based on your facts none of the decisions in question are interrelated.
³ A source is explicitly involved in a decision if the source is a claimant, applicant, respondent, contracting party, or is otherwise named or identified as the subject of the proceeding. GFV is not a claimant, applicant, respondent, contracting party, or otherwise named or identified as the subject of the proceeding.

File No. A-16-102
Page No. 5

2. Anton Development Company proposes to construct a residential building at 801 Brewster
Avenue. The Project would consist of approximately 50 very low income affordable multi-
family units and 200 market-rate units. In connection with the project, the Planning Commission
would be asked to approve a Planned Community Permit and an affordable housing plan. The
site is 1,509 feet away from the Fox Theater parcel and 1,330 feet from the Fox Forum.

3. MidPen Housing proposes to build a mixed-use building at 707 and 777 Bradford Street. The
site includes four existing parcels, including two parcels that were acquired in 2004 and 2006
with former Redwood City Redevelopment Agency Low-Moderate Housing Set Aside funds.
The project would include approximately 108 units of rental housing affordable for very low
income seniors, space for childcare or a nonprofit, and a publicly accessible park. In connection
with the project, the Planning Commission would be asked to approve a Downtown Planned
Community Permit and an affordable housing plan. The Bradford Street Affordable Housing Site
is 1,270 feet away from the Fox Theater parcel and 1,754 feet from the Fox Forum.

The nature of GFV's businesses suggest that a prudent person would not find it reasonably
foreseeable that the decisions' financial effect would contribute to a change in the value of GFV. In
looking at the example in Regulation 18702.1(b), none of these apply to the facts:

(1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an activity in which
the business entity is engaged;

(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the business entity
is engaged;

(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business entity
supplies;

(4) Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as redevelopment projects,
traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may affect, either temporarily or permanently,
the amount of business the business entity receives;

(5) Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or other project that
would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity draws from the location of the project;
or

(6) Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability of the business
entity.

While the decisions will add population to the general area, all are far enough away as to not
substantially affect GFV's business or property. Any improvements in the surrounding
neighborhood such as redevelopment projects, traffic road improvements, or parking changes that
may affect, either temporarily or permanently, would not appear to affect GFV.

Moreover, some of the projects will appear to have minimal effect on the business and
clientele of GFV's businesses. You noted that the vast majority of sales at the GFV venues in
Redwood City come through advance, online ticket sales and reservations rather than from walk-up

File No. A-16-102
Page No. 6

business. The projects would not appear to impact marketability of GFV's business. These projects
are small residential developments. For example, the closest is the Habitat for Humanity project to
construct a six-story, 25,000 square foot residential building with 20 affordable housing units. This
project will appear to have minimal impact on the area where GFV's properties are located.

This would also appear to be the case with respect to the MidPen project which is 1,270 feet
away from the Fox Theater parcel and 1,754 feet from the Fox Forum, and the Anton project located
1,509 feet away from the Fox Theater parcel and 1,330 feet from the Fox Forum. The MidPen
project involves the construction of 108 units of rental housing affordable for very low income
seniors. The Anton project would consist of approximately 50 very low income affordable multi-
family units and 200 market-rate units. Thus, none of the projects will likely have any foreseeable
financial effect on the value of GFV and the Commissioner may participate in the MidPen, Anton
and Habitat for Humanity decisions.

The Mixed-use Developments

1. Greystar Development has applied for a Planned Community Permit and Tentative Parcel Map to
construct an eight-story, approximately 346-unit multifamily residential development with
approximately 6,000 square feet of ground floor retail and two levels of underground parking at
1409 El Camino Real. Of the 346 units, 36 will be affordable housing units on-site, and Greystar
will submit a related affordable housing plan for approval to the Planning Commission. Greystar
proposes to provide approximately 403 automobile spaces for residential use, 36 automobile
spaces for retail use, and 93 bicycle spaces. The Greystar site is 1,764 feet away from the Fox
Theater parcel and 2,151 from the Fox Forum.

2. The Acclaim Companies has applied for a Planned Community Permit and a Tentative Parcel
Map to construct a four-story mixed-use office and retail building consisting of approximately
86,910 gross square feet, including approximately 6,900 square feet of retail and 80,000 square
feet of office at 851 Main Street. The project, which would combine several existing lots,
proposes to include two levels of underground parking with approximately 215 parking stalls
where 260 are required by the DTPP and proposes to pay a parking in-lieu fee for the 45 spaces
not provided on site. Acclaim also proposes to provide 56 bicycle spaces. Because the project
would exceed the office MAD Cap under the DTPP, Acclaim is seeking an amendment to the
General Plan and DTPP to allow for more office development. In addition, because the project
would exceed the DTPP MAD Cap contemplated in the DTPP EIR, a new EIR will be prepared.
The Acclaim site is 1,140 feet away from the Fox Theater parcel and 1,915 feet from the Fox
Forum.

Regulation 18702.1(b)(5) provides an example of materiality where the decision is the
location of a major development that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity
draws from the location of the project. The Greystar site is 1,764 feet away from the Fox Theater
parcel and 2,151 from the Fox Forum. They applied to construct an eight-story, approximately 346-
unit multifamily residential development with approximately 6,000 feet of ground floor retail and
two levels of underground parking at 1409 El Camino Real. Greystar proposes to provide
approximately 403 automobile spaces for residential use, 36 automobile spaces for retail use, and
93 bicycle spaces. The Acclaim site is 1,140 feet away from the Fox Theater parcel and 1,915 feet
from the Fox Forum. Acclaim intends to construct a four-story mixed-use office and retail building

File No. A-16-102
Page No. 7

consisting of approximately 86,910 gross square feet, including approximately 6,900 square feet of
retail and 80,000 square feet of office at 851 Main Street. The project will include two levels of
underground parking with approximately 215 parking stalls.

These are major developments in proximity to GFV's properties that could increase the
desirability of renting GFV facilities or attending GFC events. A prudent person with sufficient
information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would
contribute to a change in the value of GFV. Therefore, we conclude that there will be a foreseeable
and material financial effect resulting from these project decisions and the Commissioner will have
a conflict of interest and may not participate.

When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (such as a planning
commissioner) has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must:
(1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of economic interest
involved in the decision as well as the details of the economic interest on the record of the meeting; (2)
recuse himself or herself, and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on
the item. (Section 87105.)

The Historic Courthouse Square Contract.

The City's historic Courthouse Square is across the street from the GFV venues. You also
provided information regarding the distance between the proposed projects and the Courthouse
Square, however, Courthouse Square is owned by the city, not by GFV. Therefore, the square is not
a real property interest of GFV.

GFV does coordinate with the City to create large, outdoor events in the square. On June 10,
2016, you noted that for most events, GFV will collect the full amount of rent for both the Fox
Theatre and Courthouse Square as an administrative convenience. GFV retains the full revenue
associated with renting the Theatre (approximately $6,000 plus costs) and remits the full amount of
rent associated with Courthouse Square (approximately $3,900 plus costs) to the City. In other
words, for a typical event, GFV collects the entire rent for both the Theatre and the Square, but it
only earns the revenue associated with rental of the Theatre.

There is one exception to this general arrangement -- customers may rent a tent from the
City for events on Courthouse Square at a rate of $15,960, which GFV collects from the Customer.
For such tent rentals, GFV retains a 10% finders fee of $1,596 and sends the remaining $14,364 to
the City. Such tent rentals occur approximately twice a year.

None of the decisions related to any of the projects in question would appear to affect the
arrangement between the City and GFV or the revenue that GFV receives. Thus, the contractual
arrangement regarding the Courthouse Square would not create a conflict of interest in any of the
decisions described above.

File No. A-16-02
Page No. 8


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

City's Courthouse Square create a conflict of interest under any of the decisions set forth above?

decisions described above.

his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.

Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the

within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.  
• Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
GFV is an interest of the Commissioner as his employer and a source of income. (Section 
87103(c) and (d).) 
However, a conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. Note that the

the decisions are inextricably interrelated.2

1. Greystar Developme11t has applied for a Planned Community Permit and Tentative Parcel Map to 
construct an eight-story, approximately 346-unit multifamily residential development with 
approximately 6,000 square feet of ground floor retail and two levels of underground parking at 
1409 El Camino Real. Of the 346 units, 36 will be affordable housing units on-site, and Greystar 
will submit a .related affordable housing plan for approval to the PlanQing Commission. Greystar 
proposes to provide approximately 403 automobile spaces for residential use, 36 automobile 
spaces for retail use, and 93 bicycle spaces. The Grcystar site is I, 764 feet away from the Fox 
Theater parcel and 2,151 from the Fox Forum. 
2. The Acclaim Companies has applied for a Planned Community Permit and a Tentative Parcel 
Map to construct a four-story mixed-use office and retail building consisting of approximately 
86,910 gross square feet, including approximately 6,900 square feet of retail and 80,000 square 
feet of office at 851 Main Street. The project, which would combine several existing lots, 
proposes to include two levels of underground parking with approximately 215 parking stalls 
where 260 are required by the DTPP and proposes to pay a parking in-lieu fee for the 45 spaces 
not provided on site. Acclaim also proposes to provide 56 bicycle spaces. Because the project 
would exceed the office MAD Cap under the DTPP, Acclaim is seeking an amendment to the 
General Plan and DTPP to allow for more office development. In addition, because the project 
would exceed the DTPP MAD Cap contemplated in the DTPP EIR, a new EIR will be prepared. 
The Acclaim site is I, 140 feet away from the Fox Theater parcel and 1,915 feet from the Fox 
Forum. 
Regulation 18702.1 (b )(5) provides an example of materiality where the decision is the 
location of a major development that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity 
draws from the location of the project. The Greystar site is 1,764 feet away from the Fox Theater 
parcel and 2,151 from the Fox Forum. They applied to construct an eight-story, approximately 346-
unit multifamily residential development with approximately 6,000 feet of ground floor retail and 
two levels of underground parking at 1409 El Camino Real. Greystar proposes to provide 
approximately 403 automobile spaces for residential use, 36 automobile spaces for retail use, and 
93 bicycle spaces. The Acclaim site is I, 140 feet away from the Fox Theater parcel and 1,9 I 5 feet 
from the Fox Forum. Acclaim intends to construct a four-story mixed-use office and retail building  
consisting of approximately 86,910 gross square feet, including approximately 6,900 square feet of 
retail and 80,000 square feet of office at 851 Main Street. The project will include two levels of 
underground parking with approximately 215 parking stalls. 
These are major developments in proximity to GFV's properties that could increase the 
desirability of renting GFV facilities or attending GFC events. A prudent person with sufficient 
information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would 
contribute to a change in the value of GFV. Therefore, we conclude that there will be a foreseeable 
and material financial effect resulting from these project decisions and the Commissioner will have 
a conflict of interest and may not participate. 
When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (such as a planning 
commissioner) has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: 
( I )  immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of economic interest 
involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest on the record of the meeting; (2) 
recuse himself or herself, and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on 
the item. (Section 87105.) 
T/ze Historic Courthouse Square Contract. 
The City's historic Courthouse Square is across the street from the GFV venues. You also 
provided information regarding the distance between the prop
0osed projects and the Courthouse 
Square, however, Courthouse Square is owned by the city, not by GFV. Therefore, the square is not 
a real property interest of GFV. 
GFV does coordinate with the City to create large, outdoor events in the square. On June 10, 
2016, you noted that for most events, GFV will collect the full amount of rent for both the Fox 
Theatre and Courthouse Square as an administrative convenience. GFV retains the full revenue 
associated with renting the Theatre (approximately $6,000 plus costs) and remits the full amount of 
rent associated with Courthouse Square (approximately $3,900 plus costs) to the City. In other 
words, for a typical event, GFV collects the entire rent for both the Theatre and the Square, but it 
only earns the revenue associated with rental of the Theatre. 
There is one exception to this general arrangement -- customers may rent a tent from the 
City for events on Courthouse Square at a rate of $15,960, which GFV collects from the Customer. 
For such tent rentals, GFV retains a 10% finders fee of $1,596 and sends the remaining S 14,364 to 
the City. Such tent rentals occur approximately twice a year. 
None of the decisions related to any of the projects in question would appear to affect the 
arrangement between the City and GFV or the revenue that GFV receives. Thus, the contractual 
arrangement regarding the Courthouse Square would not create a conflict of interest in any of the 
decisions described above.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. May Commissioner Schmidt participate in upcoming decisions pertaining to the 
following projects (discussed in detail below): ( 1) the Habitat for Humanity project; (2) the Anton 
development; (3) the MidPen development; (4) the Greystar development; and (5) the Acclaim 
development, despite his employment with Golden Fox Venues ("GFV") which operates 
entertainment venues in the downtown area? 
2. Will the the contract between GFV and the City to conduct large, outdoor events in the 
City's Courthouse Square create a conflict of interest under any of the decisions set forth above?

CONCLUSION: 1. The Commissioner may make and participate in decisions related to the following: ( l) the 
Habitat for Humanity project; (2) the Anton development; and (3) the MidPen development. 
However, based on the scope of the Greystar development and the Acclaim development and 
potential impacts on his employer, the Commissioner may not participate in these developments.2. None of the decisions related to any of the projects in question would appear to affect the 
arrangement between the City and GFV or the revenue that GFV receives. Thus, the contractual 
arrangement regarding the Courthouse Square would not create a conflict of interest in any of the 
decisions described above.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 June 22, 2016 Jonathan W. Smith Deputy City Attorney 1017 Middlefield Road Redwood City, CA 94063 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-16-102 Dear Mr. Smith: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Planning Commissioner Ernie Schmidt regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. QUESTIONS 1. May Commissioner Schmidt participate in upcoming decisions pertaining to the following projects (discussed in detail below): (1) the Habitat for Humanity project; (2) the Anton development; (3) the MidPen development; (4) the Greystar development; and (5) the Acclaim development, despite his employment with Golden Fox Venues ("GFV") which operates entertainment venues in the downtown area? 2. Will the the contract between GFV and the City to conduct large, outdoor events in the City's Courthouse Square create a conflict of interest under any of the decisions set forth above? CONCLUSIONS 1. The Commissioner may make and participate in decisions related to the following: (1) the Habitat for Humanity project; (2) the Anton development; and (3) the MidPen development. However, based on the scope of the Greystar development and the Acclaim development and potential impacts on his employer, the Commissioner may not participate in these developments. _________________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-16-102 Page No. 2 2. None of the decisions related to any of the projects in question would appear to affect the arrangement between the City and GFV or the revenue that GFV receives. Thus, the contractual arrangement regarding the Courthouse Square would not create a conflict of interest in any of the decisions described above. FACTS You stated that Redwood City is experiencing rapid growth in its downtown area, and several development projects are expected to come before the Planning Commission in the foreseeable future. Development in the downtown area is governed by the DTPP. The DTPP establishes land use, development, and urban design regulations for the 138-acre DTPP area. It sets Maximum Allowable Development caps ("MAD Caps") of 2,500 net new dwelling units; 100,000 square feet of net new retail development; 500,000 square feet of net new commercial office space; and 200 net new lodging rooms. For each project, any net new units and/or square footage are subtracted from the amount available for other projects under the
